angle: Citation policy hardening — tie cite_results to a query-grounding / term-overlap check, not the absolute-relevance score alone, so a spurious high-cosine match cannot become a confident citation.
Citation policy hardening — tie cite_results to a query-grounding / term-overlap check, not the absolute-relevance score alone, so a spurious high-cosine match cannot become a confident citation.
title: Gate cite_results on a lexical-grounding floor, not the cosine score alone
problem: deriveCitationPolicy() at formatters/search-results.ts:368-371 returns 'cite_results' iff requestQuality.label === 'good', and that label is computed by assessRequestQuality() (lib/search/confidence-scoring.ts:433-485) from ONLY topScore (absolute cosine relevance via resolveCalibrationScore), qualityRatio, and topMargin. There is NO query-term or lexical-grounding signal anywhere in the citation path — I grepped lib/search, formatters, and handlers for grounding/term-overlap/jaccard/queryTerm and the only term-overlap logic (community-search.ts:91-98 scoreSummary, folder-discovery.ts:568-585) feeds different retrieval channels, never citation. This is exactly the 029 benchmark soft spot: off-corpus 'kubernetes' scored 0.78 good identically across all four models on the unrelated 'Spec Memory Runtime Retention Cleanup' doc (029/benchmark-results.md:71,81), passing the second good-rule at confidence-scoring.ts:476 (topScore>=0.7 AND topMargin>=0.15), so the gate emitted cite_results on a pure-cosine spurious match with zero keyword overlap. A high cosine with no lexical anchor is the textbook spurious-good. The grounding-citation angle is explicitly an un-built 030 research angle (030-vague-query-improvement-research/spec.md:67), confirming it is not yet solved.
evidence: formatters/search-results.ts:368-371 (deriveCitationPolicy keys only on label); lib/search/confidence-scoring.ts:433-485 (assessRequestQuality uses only topScore/qualityRatio/topMargin, line 476 is the rule kubernetes passes); 029/benchmark-results.md:71,81 (kubernetes 0.78 good on unrelated doc, all 4 models); search-results.ts:915 (raw fts_score/bm25_score/sourceScores.keyword already present); folder-discovery.ts:514 (extractKeywords already exported)
proposal: Add a lexical-grounding floor to the cite decision. The raw BM25/FTS score is already on every row (rawResult.fts_score / bm25_score / sourceScores.keyword — read at search-results.ts:915) and the query string is in scope at the citation call site (assessRequestQuality is called at search-results.ts:1087 and query is used 20 lines later at :1107, same function). Compute a per-top-hit grounding signal = max(query-term Jaccard/coverage over the top doc's title+triggers, normalized BM25 presence) reusing the existing extractKeywords (folder-discovery.ts:514, already exported) — no new tokenizer. Then require BOTH label==='good' AND grounding >= floor for cite_results; a good-by-cosine-only top hit with grounding below floor downgrades to do_not_cite_results (or a new 'cite_with_caveat'). Keep it additive and flag-gated (e.g. SPECKIT_CITATION_GROUNDING_FLOOR) defaulting to the current behavior until calibrated against the 029 fixtures so authentication (correctly weak) and the seven aligned good queries are unaffected.
benefit: Closes the one confirmed false-good in the 144-cell benchmark: kubernetes@0.78 would have zero/low keyword overlap with the retention-cleanup doc and fail the floor, flipping to do_not_cite while authentication and the genuinely-grounded good queries (graph, memory, routing, scores) keep citing. Cite-iff-good fidelity stayed perfect across models, so hardening the underlying gate, not the model contract, is the highest-leverage fix.
risk: A grounding floor that is too aggressive could suppress legitimately-citable semantic-only matches where the corpus uses different vocabulary than the query (true synonyms). Mitigate by OR-ing lexical overlap with a high cosine ceiling (e.g. still cite at cosine>=0.9 even with low overlap) and calibrating the floor against the 029 golden set so the seven aligned good queries do not regress.

angle: Citation policy hardening — tie cite_results to a query-grounding / term-overlap check, not the absolute-relevance score alone, so a spurious high-cosine match cannot become a confident citation.
title: Surface a grounding field in the envelope so the citation downgrade is legible, not silent
problem: When the score-only gate currently waves a spurious good through, the envelope carries citationPolicy='cite_results' plus a high confidence.value with no signal that the match is lexically ungrounded. The per-result confidence drivers (confidence-scoring.ts:402-405) only ever surface upward drivers (large_margin, multi_channel_agreement, anchor_density) — there is no 'low_grounding' or 'cosine_only' negative driver, and the response envelope (search-results.ts:1167-1179) emits requestQuality + citationPolicy but no grounding evidence. So even a human reviewing the 029 kubernetes output sees only '0.78 good, cite_results' with nothing flagging that the top doc shares no query terms. The 029 benchmark itself had to manually identify the unrelated doc by name (benchmark-results.md:81) precisely because the envelope exposed no grounding signal.
evidence: lib/search/confidence-scoring.ts:402-405 (drivers only positive, no grounding/negative driver); formatters/search-results.ts:1167-1179 (envelope emits requestQuality+citationPolicy, no grounding field); 029/benchmark-results.md:81 (the unrelated doc had to be identified manually, envelope gave no signal)
proposal: When Proposal 1's grounding floor is computed, also emit it as an additive envelope field (e.g. requestQuality.grounding: {topHitOverlap: number, lexicalSupport: 'strong'|'weak'|'none'}) and add a 'low_grounding' negative driver to the per-result confidence drivers array when the top hit is cosine-only. This makes any future spurious-good self-documenting without changing the cite decision on its own.
benefit: Turns a silent calibration failure into an inspectable one: a downgraded or borderline citation now carries the why, models and humans can see 'good but ungrounded', and the field becomes a cheap regression probe for future benchmark runs (the next 029-style sweep can assert grounding>=floor on every good verdict).
risk: Adds a field to the response contract; the 029 finding that some models drop requestQuality (benchmark-results.md:63-69 null verdicts) means a nested grounding sub-field may also be dropped by lower-fidelity models. Keep it strictly additive and optional so a dropped field degrades to today's behavior rather than breaking consumers.

angle: Citation policy hardening — tie cite_results to a query-grounding / term-overlap check, not the absolute-relevance score alone, so a spurious high-cosine match cannot become a confident citation.
title: Replace the binary cite/do-not-cite split with an explicit cite_with_caveat tier for borderline grounding
problem: CitationPolicy is a hard binary — 'cite_results' | 'do_not_cite_results' (formatters/search-results.ts:218), derived by a single equality test (deriveCitationPolicy :368-371). This forces a grounding gate (Proposal 1) into an all-or-nothing flip: a top hit that is strongly relevant by cosine but only weakly grounded lexically must be either fully cited (today's spurious-good risk) or fully suppressed (over-suppression risk noted in Proposal 1). There is no middle rung even though the upstream requestQuality already has three tiers (good/weak/gap, confidence-scoring.ts:100) and the response side already models a graduated responsePolicy with broaden_or_ask vs refuse (search-results.ts:217,360-365). The binary citation policy is the only part of the chain that lost the gradient.
evidence: formatters/search-results.ts:218 (CitationPolicy is a 2-value union); :368-371 (single-equality derivation, no middle tier); :217,360-365 (responsePolicy already graduated with broaden_or_ask vs refuse); lib/search/confidence-scoring.ts:100 (requestQuality already 3-tier good/weak/gap)
proposal: Extend CitationPolicy to a third value 'cite_with_caveat' (or 'cite_with_grounding_warning'). Map: label==='good' AND grounding>=floor -> cite_results; label==='good' AND grounding<floor -> cite_with_caveat (cite but instruct the consumer to hedge / verify the path); not good -> do_not_cite_results. This lets the grounding floor (Proposal 1) be tuned conservatively, a borderline match is hedged rather than dropped, preserving recall for genuine synonym matches while still not asserting a spurious good with full confidence.
benefit: Removes the false dichotomy that makes the grounding floor risky: high-cosine-low-overlap matches get a hedge instead of a hard drop, so legitimate cross-vocabulary hits survive while spurious-goods like kubernetes lose their unqualified citation. Mirrors the already-graduated responsePolicy, keeping the two policies coherent.
risk: Adds a third enum value to a contract that 029 showed some models already render imperfectly; a model that does not understand cite_with_caveat may treat it as cite or as do_not_cite unpredictably. Mitigate by documenting it as 'cite but hedge' and defaulting consumers that do not recognize it to the safe do_not_cite interpretation; ship behind the same flag as Proposal 1 so the binary remains the default until the tier is validated against the benchmark.
